purpose: SAI-to-vendor-SDK bridge for ASIC programming. Receives SAI API calls from orchagent (via ASIC_DB redis notifications), translates them into vendor-specific SDK calls, and programs the forwarding ASIC. Acts as the sole gateway between the SONiC control plane and physical hardware. Owns ASIC_DB as the source of truth for hardware object state.

daemons.name: syncd
daemons.role: Main SAI bridge daemon. Subscribes to ASIC_DB for orchagent-written SAI object entries, calls the vendor SAI shared library (libsai.so) to program the ASIC, and writes SAI object OIDs and counter data back to ASIC_DB and COUNTERS_DB. Hosts the flex-counter thread that periodically polls ASIC counters and populates COUNTERS_DB for telemetry and monitoring. Single-threaded for SAI calls (SAI is not thread-safe); counter polling runs on a separate thread.

reads: ASIC_DB: ASIC_STATE:* (SAI object create/set/remove/get requests from orchagent)
FLEX_COUNTER_DB: FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE (counter polling configuration and intervals)
writes: ASIC_DB: ASIC_STATE:* (SAI OIDs after object creation)
ASIC_DB: RIDTABLE (RIF/object ID mapping for warm restart)
COUNTERS_DB: COUNTERS:* (port, queue, pg, ACL, tunnel counters)

daemons.name: dsserve
daemons.role: Debug shell server. Provides a Unix socket-based debug REPL that allows engineers to issue raw SAI API calls interactively for diagnostic purposes. Used by 'bcmcmd' or vendor-equivalent debug tools. Not involved in the forwarding plane data path; for diagnostic access only.

daemons.name: gbsyncd
daemons.role: Gearbox syncd daemon. Platform-dependent -- only present on systems with external PHY gearbox devices (e.g., certain 400G platforms with retimers). Provides a secondary SAI bridge specifically for gearbox PHY programming. Mirrors the syncd pattern but targets the gearbox SAI library. Reads from GB_ASIC_DB and writes counter data to GB_COUNTERS_DB.

key_data_flows.path: orchagent writes ASIC_DB:ASIC_STATE:<SAI_OBJECT_TYPE>:<temp_OID> -> syncd receives redis keyspace notification -> syncd calls libsai.so sai_*_api->create_* -> vendor SDK programs ASIC -> syncd writes real OID back to ASIC_DB
key_data_flows.notes: The entire ASIC programming path runs through this single channel. If ASIC_DB pub/sub is broken or syncd is slow, all hardware programming stalls. Use 'redis-cli -n 1 monitor' to observe real-time SAI calls.

key_data_flows.path: FLEX_COUNTER_DB:FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE configures polling intervals -> syncd flex-counter thread wakes on interval -> calls SAI sai_port_api->get_port_stats() in batch -> writes raw counter values to COUNTERS_DB:COUNTERS:<port_OID>
key_data_flows.notes: Counter polling is independent of SAI programming. A hung flex-counter thread does NOT block SAI calls but COUNTERS_DB goes stale. 'show interfaces counters' will show frozen values.

key_data_flows.path: syncd starts -> calls sai_switch_api->get_switch_attribute() for capability set -> writes results to STATE_DB:SWITCH_CAPABILITY|switch -> orchagent reads on startup to constrain what SAI objects it will attempt to create
key_data_flows.notes: If this flow fails, orchagent may attempt unsupported SAI operations and get SAI_STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED errors. Check STATE_DB:SWITCH_CAPABILITY|switch is populated after syncd start.

key_data_flows.path: orchagent writes GB_ASIC_DB:ASIC_STATE:* -> gbsyncd receives notification -> calls gearbox SAI library -> programs external PHY via platform-specific bus
key_data_flows.notes: Only active on gearbox-capable platforms. If gbsyncd is absent on a non-gearbox platform, GB_ASIC_DB will be empty and this flow is a no-op.

warm_restart.mechanism: syncd saves ASIC object state (OID mappings, RID table) to ASIC_DB:RIDTABLE before exit. On restart, syncd calls sai_switch_api->initialize_switch() in warm-boot mode, which instructs the vendor SDK to reconnect to the running ASIC without a hardware reset. orchagent then reconciles ASIC_DB state against APPL_DB and only programs deltas.
warm_restart.caveat: Warm restart requires vendor SAI and SDK support. Not all platforms support SAI warm boot. A failed SAI warm boot will cause syncd to fall back to cold boot, resetting all ASIC state and triggering full reprogramming by orchagent. Verify with platform vendor before relying on this in production.

restart_impact: Severe traffic disruption. A syncd restart triggers a full ASIC reset on most platforms (cold boot), wiping all programmed routes, neighbors, VLANs, ACLs, and port configurations from hardware. orchagent must then fully reprogram all objects. On large-scale deployments with 500k+ routes and thousands of ACL entries, convergence can take 30-120 seconds. With warm restart enabled and vendor support, disruption is reduced to the SAI reconnect window (typically 1-5 seconds).

restart_commands.syncd_only: docker exec syncd supervisorctl restart syncd
restart_commands.gbsyncd_only: docker exec syncd supervisorctl restart gbsyncd
restart_commands.warm_restart: config warm_restart enable syncd && sudo systemctl restart syncd

health_check_commands: docker exec syncd ps aux | grep syncd
state_verification_commands: redis-cli -n 1 keys 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_ROUTE_ENTRY*' | wc -l (ASIC_DB programmed route count)
redis-cli -n 1 keys 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_NEIGHBOR_ENTRY*' | wc -l (ASIC_DB programmed neighbor count)
redis-cli -n 1 keys 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_PORT*' | wc -l (ASIC_DB port object count)
redis-cli -n 2 hgetall 'COUNTERS:oid:0x*' (verify COUNTERS_DB is being populated)
sonic-db-cli STATE_DB hgetall 'SWITCH_CAPABILITY|switch' (verify SAI capabilities advertised)
show interfaces counters (verify counter collection is live, not frozen)

log_locations.vendor_sdk: Platform-dependent. Common: /var/log/bcm.log (Broadcom), /var/log/sai.log (generic SAI trace). Check /etc/sonic/syncd.conf for SDK log path overrides.

symptoms: All hardware forwarding stops simultaneously -- all traffic blackholed
supervisorctl shows syncd in FATAL or STOPPED state
systemctl status syncd shows recent restart timestamps in rapid succession
ASIC_DB ASIC_STATE keys are present but hardware is not programmed
orchagent.log shows 'SAI error' or 'syncd restarted' messages
show interfaces status shows all ports as operationally down after restart
causes: Vendor SAI library (libsai.so) assertion or segfault triggered by an unsupported object combination or scale limit
Vendor SDK initialization failure -- SDK cannot communicate with ASIC (PCI enumeration failure, firmware mismatch)
OOM killer terminating syncd during large route batch programming under memory pressure
ASIC_DB contains stale or corrupt SAI objects from a prior crash that trigger invariant violations in SAI
Kernel module for ASIC driver not loaded or wrong version loaded
Platform hardware fault (ASIC unresponsive over PCIe)
diagnosis: tail -200 /var/log/syncd.log -- look for 'SAI_STATUS_FAILURE', 'assert', 'SIGSEGV', 'terminate called after throwing'
ls -lt /var/core/*.core | head -5 -- check for fresh syncd core files
dmesg | grep -iE 'oom|killed|syncd|pci|asic' -- check for OOM kill or PCIe errors
journalctl -u syncd --since '10 minutes ago' | grep -iE 'error|fail|crash'
redis-cli -n 1 dbsize -- check if ASIC_DB is unexpectedly large (scale trigger)
docker exec syncd tail -50 /var/log/syncd.log | grep -i 'sai_status'
lsmod | grep -i asic (or platform kernel module name) -- verify ASIC driver loaded
resolution: For SAI library crash: capture core file and syncd.log, open vendor TAC case with both artifacts
For stale ASIC_DB: redis-cli -n 1 FLUSHDB (WARNING: clears all ASIC state) then sudo systemctl restart syncd && swss -- causes full reprogramming
For OOM: show processes memory | grep syncd; reduce scale or increase available memory; check for leak with repeated 'show processes memory' over time
For SDK init failure: check dmesg for ASIC driver errors; verify correct platform drivers loaded with 'sudo platform_utils.sh' or equivalent; power-cycle may be required
For kernel module: sudo modprobe <asic_driver_module>; verify with lsmod

symptoms: ASIC_DB shows routes/neighbors/VLANs present but traffic is not forwarded as expected
orchagent.log shows successful ASIC_DB writes with no errors
syncd.log shows SAI calls completing with SAI_STATUS_SUCCESS but hardware behavior is wrong
Specific traffic class or prefix range is affected while others work (partial divergence)
show arp shows neighbors resolved but pings to those destinations fail
ASIC counters show packets arriving on ingress port but no egress (ASIC drop, not Linux drop)
causes: syncd SAI call returned SAI_STATUS_SUCCESS but vendor SDK silently deferred or dropped the programming (known SDK bugs on some platforms)
orchagent wrote SAI objects in wrong dependency order -- child object created before parent (e.g., route before next-hop OID resolved)
ASIC hardware table corruption requiring SDK or ASIC reset to clear
Mismatched SAI library version between syncd binary and installed libsai.so -- silent API semantic differences
Flex-counter polling interfering with SAI programming thread on platforms with non-reentrant SAI implementations
diagnosis: redis-cli -n 1 keys 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_ROUTE_ENTRY*x.x.x.x*' -- verify specific route is in ASIC_DB
docker exec syncd grep 'x.x.x.x' /var/log/syncd.log | grep -i 'sai_status' -- check SAI return code for that route
show platform sai-profile (if available) -- verify SAI library version matches expected
redis-cli -n 1 monitor (in a separate shell) while re-adding a test route -- watch for SAI call and response
Use vendor diagnostic tools (e.g., 'bcmcmd l3 defip show' for Broadcom) to inspect ASIC hardware tables directly
show interfaces counters (look for RX OK but no TX on expected egress ports -- ASIC-level drop)
resolution: For silent SDK failures: restart syncd (sudo systemctl restart syncd && swss) to force full reprogram; if recurring, file vendor bug with syncd.log and ASIC hardware dump
For dependency ordering: usually an orchagent bug; check orchagent.log for 'retry' entries; typically resolves after orchagent retry loop completes
For ASIC table corruption: platform-dependent; may require 'sudo syncd_reset' or platform reboot
For SAI version mismatch: verify with 'docker exec syncd syncd --version' and 'ls -la /usr/lib/libsai.so*'; reinstall correct SAI package

symptoms: syncd RSS memory grows continuously over hours or days without bound
show processes memory | grep syncd shows steadily increasing memory usage across checks
System free memory decreases over time even under stable routing table
Eventually syncd is OOM-killed, causing full ASIC reset and traffic outage
Memory growth correlates with route churn events (BGP reconvergence, link flaps) or counter polling activity
causes: Route churn (mass withdraw/re-add cycles) causes SAI object create/delete pairs that leak internal SDK memory structures
Counter polling (flex-counter thread) accumulates OID list entries without cleanup when ports are removed or recreated
Large-scale deployments with many SAI objects exhaust syncd internal hash table memory over time
Vendor SAI library memory leak in specific API call paths (especially sai_route_entry or sai_next_hop_group_member APIs)
ASIC_DB subscriber thread leaking redis reply objects on high-churn key notification streams
diagnosis: watch -n 30 'show processes memory | grep syncd' -- observe trend over time
redis-cli -n 1 dbsize -- check if ASIC_DB is growing unexpectedly (orphaned objects)
redis-cli -n 5 keys '*' | wc -l -- check FLEX_COUNTER_DB for orphaned counter entries
redis-cli -n 1 keys 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_NEXT_HOP_GROUP_MEMBER*' | wc -l -- ECMP member leak is common
grep -c 'sai_route_entry' /var/log/syncd.log -- high SAI route call count may indicate churn-driven leak
valgrind --tool=massif (only in lab) or use vendor SAI diagnostic memory dump tools
resolution: Immediate: if OOM is imminent, schedule a maintenance window and restart syncd during low-traffic period
Reduce route churn: investigate BGP instability with 'show bgp summary' and 'show bgp dampening parameters'; dampen flapping peers
For counter leak: audit FLEX_COUNTER_DB for orphaned entries: redis-cli -n 5 keys 'FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE*' | xargs -I{} redis-cli -n 5 del {} (remove stale entries); then restart syncd
For vendor SAI leak: upgrade SAI library to latest vendor-recommended version; document leak rate and file vendor bug
Long-term: implement scheduled syncd restart during maintenance window (config cron) if leak rate is known and bounded

symptoms: show interfaces counters output shows the same values across repeated calls -- counters are frozen
COUNTERS_DB timestamps are not updating
Monitoring systems (Prometheus, SNMP) show flat-line counter graphs with no increments
CPU usage for syncd is either abnormally high (busy loop) or zero (thread stopped)
Other syncd functionality (SAI programming) may still work -- traffic forwarding is unaffected
redis-cli -n 2 hgetall 'COUNTERS:oid:0x*' returns stale values
causes: Vendor SAI sai_port_api->get_port_stats() call blocking indefinitely in SDK (firmware hang during counter read DMA)
FLEX_COUNTER_DB misconfiguration -- counter polling interval set to 0 or invalid value causing tight poll loop that starves syncd main thread
OID reference in FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE points to a deleted or invalid SAI object causing SAI to return SAI_STATUS_ITEM_NOT_FOUND in a tight retry loop
syncd main thread deadlocked with flex-counter thread over a shared SAI API mutex (SAI is not thread-safe on some platforms)
COUNTERS_DB redis connection timeout or backpressure from large counter write batch
diagnosis: redis-cli -n 2 hgetall COUNTERS_DB_VERSION -- check if COUNTERS_DB is being updated at all

date; redis-cli -n 2 hget 'COUNTERS:oid:0x1000000000002' SAI_PORT_STAT_IF_IN_OCTETS; sleep 10; redis-cli -n 2 hget 'COUNTERS:oid:0x1000000000002' SAI_PORT_STAT_IF_IN_OCTETS -- compare two values to confirm stale
docker exec syncd top -b -n 1 | grep syncd -- check CPU consumption of syncd process
redis-cli -n 5 hgetall 'FLEX_COUNTER_GROUP_TABLE|PORT_STAT_COUNTER_POLLING_INTERVAL' -- verify polling interval is reasonable (default 1000ms)
redis-cli -n 5 keys 'FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE*' | wc -l -- check for OID list bloat
strace -p $(pgrep syncd) -e trace=futex,nanosleep (lab only) -- detect deadlock or spin-wait
resolution: Immediate relief: docker exec syncd supervisorctl restart syncd -- resets flex-counter thread state; NOTE: this also resets ASIC and triggers full reprogram
For tight poll loop: redis-cli -n 5 hset 'FLEX_COUNTER_GROUP_TABLE|PORT_STAT_COUNTER_POLLING_INTERVAL' POLL_INTERVAL 1000 -- reset to 1000ms default
For orphaned OIDs: redis-cli -n 5 keys 'FLEX_COUNTER_TABLE*PORT*' | xargs redis-cli -n 5 del -- remove stale counter entries, then let orchagent repopulate
For vendor SAI blocking in get_port_stats: reduce counter polling frequency as workaround; file vendor bug with syncd.log showing last SAI call before hang
To monitor after fix: watch -n 5 'show interfaces counters | head -20' -- verify counters are incrementing

scale_considerations.max_sai_objects: Platform-dependent hard limit enforced by vendor SAI. syncd returns SAI_STATUS_TABLE_FULL when exceeded. Check 'show platform sai-profile' and vendor capacity documentation. Common limits: 32k IPv4 routes (low-end), 256k+ (high-end datacenter ASICs).
scale_considerations.counter_polling_overhead: Each polled counter requires a SAI get_stats() call. On systems with 128+ ports and 100+ counters per port, each poll cycle issues 12,800+ SAI calls. Default 1-second polling on large systems can create measurable SAI bus contention. Tune FLEX_COUNTER_GROUP_TABLE polling intervals if SAI latency increases.
scale_considerations.asic_db_size: ASIC_DB grows proportionally with programmed objects. Redis memory usage of 500MB+ is normal on 400k-route deployments. Monitor with 'redis-cli -n 1 info memory'. Unexplained growth indicates orphaned SAI objects from a prior crash.
scale_considerations.warm_boot_timing: SAI warm boot reconciliation time scales with ASIC_DB size. 400k routes may take 30+ seconds for syncd to fully reconcile. orchagent will stall on ASIC_DB subscription during this window -- normal behavior, not a hang.
